food_subgroup: Noodle soup with braised or stewed pork

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mee Tai Mak Pork Leg Soup. Closest parent 'pork soup (food)' used as anchor. The dish also contains rice-flour noodles which are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this composite noodle-soup dish. A0CKP (Pork, cooked) is used as the closest defensible parent anchor for the dominant protein component. The rice-flour noodle and broth matrix are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish or its closest composite food category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Mee Tai Mak Pork Leg Soup weighs approximately 450 g, comprising rice-flour noodles (~120 g), braised pork leg pieces (~130 g), and pork broth (~200 g).

nutrient_source.title: Pork leg soup with rice noodles — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for braised pork leg and rice noodle soup components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for pork leg and rice noodle reference values
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for the exact composite dish 'Mee Tai Mak Pork Leg Soup (Banh Canh Gio Heo)'. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) HPB Foodo entry for 'Pork leg, braised' (per 100 g cooked), (2) HPB Foodo entry for 'Rice noodles, cooked' (per 100 g), and (3) USDA SR Legacy entry for 'Pork broth / stock'. Weighted average applied based on approximate bowl composition (pork leg ~29%, rice noodles ~27%, broth ~44%). Values represent the composite per-100 g profile of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles (cooked) as analogous GI anchor; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish 'Mee Tai Mak Pork Leg Soup'. Mee Tai Mak are short, thick rice-flour noodles closely analogous to rice noodles (cooked). The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 report GI of rice noodles (cooked) at approximately 53 (low GI). The protein and fat content of the pork leg and broth components further attenuate postprandial glycaemic response. GI of 53 is applied as a conservative analogous estimate for the composite dish. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 41.4) / 100 ≈ 22.1 g per 450 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g bowl provides approximately 1,890 mg sodium, which represents 82–94% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit the soup liquid consumed, and avoid adding additional fish sauce or soy sauce at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load from rice-flour noodles offset by protein and fat from pork leg
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycaemic load of approximately 22 g per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the noodle component, pair with non-starchy vegetables where available, and be aware that the high sodium content of the broth presents an additional cardiometabolic risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, saturated fat from pork leg skin and collagen-rich broth, and dietary cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~1,890 mg per serving), saturated fat (~6.3 g per serving), and dietary cholesterol (~171 mg per serving) makes this dish a high-concern food for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be limited to occasional meals, the skin and fatty portions of the pork leg should be avoided, and the broth volume should be reduced.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Tai Mak Pork Leg Soup, also known by its Vietnamese name Bánh Canh Giò Heo, is a hearty noodle soup dish served in Singapore, particularly at Vietnamese-influenced eateries such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The dish features mee tai mak — short, thick, slightly chewy rice-flour noodles with a smooth, slippery texture — submerged in a rich, collagen-laden pork bone broth that has been simmered for several hours. The centrepiece protein is braised pork leg (gio heo), which is slow-cooked until the meat is tender and the skin becomes gelatinous. The broth is seasoned with fish sauce, garlic, shallots, and white pepper, giving it a savoury, umami-forward flavour with mild sweetness from the pork bones. The dish is typically garnished with spring onion, fried shallots, and fresh chilli. Nutritionally, the composite dish provides approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution (~7.8 g/100 g) from the pork leg and a moderate carbohydrate load (~9.2 g/100 g) from the rice-flour noodles. The glycaemic index is estimated at 53 (low GI) based on the rice noodle component, though the glycaemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 22 g. The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1,890 mg per bowl), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, making it suitable for those with gluten intolerance. It is not halal due to the pork content. This dish is popular as a lunch or dinner option in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant segment and reflects the culinary influence of Vietnamese immigrant communities on Singapore's hawker and restaurant food culture.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo component entries for braised pork leg and cooked rice noodles, weighted by approximate bowl composition, and supplemented by USDA SR Legacy for broth. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of this dish as served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant would be required for high-confidence values. 2. Sodium content is particularly variable depending on the restaurant's broth recipe and seasoning practices; the value of 420 mg/100 g is a mid-range estimate. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entries used for this composite estimate. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in pork-based dishes per USDA SR Legacy reference data. 7. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 8. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles; the actual GI of the composite dish may be lower due to the attenuating effect of protein and fat from the pork leg and broth. 9. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 41.4 g carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 22.1 g.
